channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Monte Cook Games' Jewel in the Sky Brings Sci-Fi Fantasy Megadungeon to the Cypher System
item.content:encoded:
Monte Cook Games has launched a new crowdfunding campaign. The new Jewel in the Sky Backerkit campaign introduces an original dungeon crawl experience, blending elements of fantasy magic and sci-fi technology.

The Jewel in the Sky Backerkit Campaign

According to its campaign page, Jewel in the Sky is a TTRPG campaign where players explore a gigantic floating island in the sky known as Vocurro. It's an island full of mysterious passages, unusual denizens known as visitors, and a strange, gravity-defying mineral that can power airships and other fantastic items.

Much like The Darkest Woods, Jewel in the Sky is setting-agnostic, allowing GMs to add it to any game with relative ease. The book contains two versions of the Vocurro megadungeon. One is fantasy-themed, where the flying island errs more on the magical and the whimsical. The other is sci-fi-themed, making the experience feel like you're exploring an abandoned alien megastructure.

Regardless of how you do it, the dungeon contains ideas and scenarios that mix and blend sci-fi and fantasy concepts. What happens when ghosts and fusion radiation intermingle? How does an abandoned AI cope on a planet where magic and spellcraft exist? The list goes on.

Notably, Jewel in the Sky marks the return of an industry legend to making a dungeon crawl. Monte Cook, whose body of work includes a ton of Dungeons & Dragons 3rd Edition supplements, is a lead designer on this book. In addition, it marks the first original dungeon crawl experience for the Cypher System, the studio's original award-winning TTRPG system.

The different pledge levels for Jewel in the Sky are as follows:

  • Standard Edition Book (PDF) for $19: A PDF of the campaign
  • Standard Edition + Cypher (PDF) for $39: Digital copies of the campaign, and the Cypher System Character Rulebook and GM Guide
  • Jewel on VTT for $49: Access to the campaign on either Roll20 or Alchemy RPG
  • Standard Edition Book (Print and PDF) for $54: Physical and digital copies of the campaign
  • Standard Edition + Cypher (Print and PDF) for $104: Digital and print copies of the campaign and the Cypher System Second Edition Character Rulebook and GM Guide
  • Deluxe Boxed Set for $149: Physical and digital copies of the game and an exclusive deluxe boxed edition, which contains poster maps, table maps, physical props and handouts, cypher, creature, and intrusion cards, custom character sheets, and a twin clock timer
  • Deluxe Boxed Set + Cypher (Print and PDF) for $209: Everything in the Deluxe Boxed Set pledge level, digital copies of the Cypher System Character Rulebook and GM Guide, and a physical Cypher Corebook slip case

At the time of writing, the Jewel in the Sky campaign has raised $119,835, more than double its initial funding goal of $50,000. The campaign ends on May 1, 2026.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler

    Additional topics:

  • Announcement

  • RPG



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/monte-cook-games-jewel-in-sky-brings-sci-fi-fantasy-megadungeon-to-cypher-system
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Last Caretaker Preview - Saving Humanity, One Dismantled Table At a Time
item.content:encoded:
For better or worse, survival crafting games have accounted for a large portion of Steam Early Access releases since its inception. The Last Caretaker aims to take the ever-popular genre in its own distinct direction, as you control a lone robot in a flooded world and perhaps save humanity.

A Lonely World

The titular last caretaker is a cheerful-looking robot that wakes up in an abandoned facility. Without any memories, their only guide is a friendly voice that occasionally chimes in with new observations. Making your way through each room, you’ll learn more about the basic gameplay loop, using cables to reroute power and scrapping everything you can see for materials.

You eventually open a way out of the facility, commandeering a boat that becomes your mobile base of operations. It’s a solid tutorial, letting you figure out how things work without holding you hand — fitting for a game where you’re pretty much on your own narratively.

Rather than a completely open sandbox, The Last Caretaker, in its current form, is a directed experience. Quests push you towards new key locations, and your level determines what crafting recipes you have access to. With XP earned frequently as you complete quests, loot items, and take on enemies, your newly unlocked recipes usually line up with what you’ll actually need to complete the next objective.

Along the way, you build up a reliable source of power on the boat. Most electricity comes from renewable sources like turbines for wind or solar panels, though you can use petrol in a pinch. You connect everything up via cable and pipes, using extenders to link multiple devices together. My cable setup on the boat was never perfect, though it doesn’t take much work to store more power than you’ll ever need.

Simple Survival

Currently, there’s little in the way of survival mechanics despite the genre. You have health, but it only goes down from taking direct damage, and a power meter that you top up at any powered outlet. Your max power and efficiency are also stats that can be improved via levelling up, and I found that it stopped being much of a limiter after a few hours.

Most of the survival comes in the form of combat, which is serviceable for the most part. Enemies are generally small robots or sentient blobs of goo that die in one hit to melee attacks, and you can craft an electric-powered pistol to zap groups of them at once. More gun crafting recipes unlock at higher levels, along with extra attachments like scopes or barrels.

You’ll only encounter two larger enemy types (plus robot sharks, though these are mostly passive), and they’re not the most fun to fight. Both are giant metal balls that continuously fire at you once you’re in range. While not tough to take out, there’s little in the way of visual feedback as you dump multiple magazines of ammo into them before they finally go down.

At the very least, the lack of many difficult enemies makes for chill resource gathering. Most objects that aren’t bolted down can be dismantled into scrap, which is then refined further into usable materials. It’s easy to end up with piles of hundreds of materials, though crafting will thankfully automatically use anything in the nearby area, even if it’s not in storage.

Shortly into your journey, you’re pointed towards another large facility. This is where the main hook of the narrative starts, as you unlock the ability to grow your very own humans. That’s right, rather than finding surviving humans, you’re instead creating new life entirely.

By using human mementos (ranging from children’s art to copies of The Art of War) and processing food, you can adjust the stats of the finalized human. From there, you’ll send them up into space via a large rocket. While certainly an interesting twist on the usual survival crafting goals you’d see in other games, it’s also my main problem with The Last Caretaker right now.

The Last Caretaker is Perhaps a Little Too Early

In its current Early Access launch state, The Last Caretaker is missing most of the content mentioned on the game’s Steam page. There’s no vast ocean world (you can visit around 30 locations, though only a few are truly unique), deep crafting, and your choices definitely don’t “shape the narrative”. There are references to mechanics like automated defenses and sieges too, none of which exist in any form right now.

And I get it, Early Access means that content is naturally going to be missing. My issue with The Last Caretaker at the moment is that the current main story ends before you really get a proper sense of how the game is going to progress after the opening section.

You have to send up at least one human to finish the current main quest, but you’re not given any context as to what the different types of created humans will actually do. Aspects like your ship also feel like they have little room for growth, due to a lack of meaningful upgrades or any real customisation. 

It’s a shame, as I enjoyed most of my 20 or so hours with The Last Caretaker during my initial playthrough. But I’m not sure if ending things at this current point is the best idea. Without the randomised worlds of Valheim or the detailed base creation of Enshrouded, there’s very little to keep you going between Early Access updates.

The Last Caretaker Preview | Final Thoughts

For now, I’d only really recommend The Last Caretaker if you’re okay with a relatively short main story with the potential for more depth later down the line. Otherwise, it might be better to wait and see what the developers are cooking up during Early Access.

The Last Caretaker was previewed on PC via Steam over 30 hours using a copy provided by the developer. 

Isaac is the Guides Editor at TechRaptor, handling guides — and the occasional review — for games throughout many genres. Some of his more extensive work at… More about Isaac



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/last-caretaker-preview
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: SpaceCraft Early Access Release Date Readies for Launch in May
item.content:encoded:
Developer and publisher Shiro Games has announced the Early Access release date for ambitious space sandbox sim SpaceCraft, and if you've got room for another one of these games in your life, you'll want to mark your calendar for May.

As revealed during today's Triple-I Initiative indie showcase, Shiro will be releasing SpaceCraft into Early Access on May 20th, which gives you just under six weeks to prepare yourself for its arrival. Hopefully, you'll be done with No Man's Sky's Xeno Arena update by then.

In SpaceCraft, Shiro is promising a "vast, seamless universe spanning multiple star systems" in which you can "freely transition from deep space travel to planetary exploration". You can use those systems to build yourself an intergalactic production empire that will turn you into a "major industrial force".

Although what you've heard so far might make SpaceCraft sound like the aforementioned other space sandbox game, this one marks itself out by providing the opportunity for players to create and run corporations in order to collaborate with others and "coordinate large-scale operations".

Shiro CEO Nicolas Cannasse says SpaceCraft's goal is to give players "the tools to shape a living, player-driven universe at their own pace", and that "every decision contributes to a larger ecosystem", whether those decisions are based on exploration, industry, or trade.

During the course of its time in Early Access, SpaceCraft will be updated with a number of new systems and features, including the likes of combat, new social options for corporations, and new sectors and planets, plus lots more.

You can check out the new Early Access release date trailer for SpaceCraft, which shows off some of the game's impressive-looking interlocking systems, right here.

SpaceCraft launches on PC via Steam Early Access on May 20th. Shiro says it'll stay in Early Access "for at least six months", but that it could be there for longer, so don't expect a quick 1.0 for this one.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/spacecraft-early-access-release-date
